stimulus: The composer Aram Tovmasyan grew up in Yerevan, immigrated to France as a young adult, and studied with teachers steeped in both Armenian folk traditions and the European avant-garde. In his 1958 String Quartet No. 3, Tovmasyan fuses these influences into a musical language so personal that it resists any attempt to assign the piece to a single national or stylistic tradition. Composers, he often insisted, should pursue an individual voice even as they draw on the music of the cultures around them.
stem: Which quotation from a music critic most directly challenges the underlined claim in the text?

answerOptions.content: “Although several Caucasian folk traditions made use of modal scales, the scalar passages running through the slow movement of String Quartet No. 3 point to a specifically Armenian source.”
“In String Quartet No. 3, the blending of folk and avant-garde idioms functions as a musical analogue to the work’s subject — a meditation on a life lived across multiple traditions.”
“Many critics emphasize the Armenian elements in String Quartet No. 3 and other key works by Tovmasyan at the expense of the equally important European avant-garde influences.”

“Though String Quartet No. 3 features clear avant-garde gestures, it is dominated by the modal melodies and rhythmic patterns of Armenian folk music — features so central that the work belongs squarely within the Armenian folk-derived tradition.”

rationale:
Choice D is correct. This critic directly challenges the underlined claim by arguing that String Quartet No. 3 is not so personal as to resist classification — on the contrary, its dominant features place it “squarely within the Armenian folk-derived tradition.” This implies the work can be classified through a single cultural lens, contradicting the claim that it resists such classification.

Choice A is incorrect because, although it identifies an Armenian element in one section of the work, it does not claim that the entire work can be assigned to a single tradition. Choice B is incorrect because it supports rather than challenges the underlined claim, agreeing that the work blends multiple traditions. Choice C is incorrect because it also supports the claim by arguing that critics should view the work’s influences as equally balanced rather than reducible to one tradition.
